---
title: "Publications"
---

## Selected Publications

### Journal Articles

**Walker, Robert W.**, and Tim Johnson. 2018. "The Career Advancement of Military Veterans in Recent Cohorts of the U.S. Executive Branch." *Public Personnel Management* 47(4): 367-394.

::: {.callout-note}
## Abstract
To measure the effect of veterans' preference on U.S. federal workforce quality, researchers have assessed whether military veterans advance in their federal careers at a different rate than nonveterans. This research, however, has produced mixed results.
:::

[PDF](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/pdf/10.1177/0091026018783015) | [Supplementary Materials](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/suppl/10.1177/0091026018783015)

---

Rizova, Polly S., Elliot N. Maltz, Samir Gupta, and **Robert W. Walker**. 2018. "Overcoming equivocality on projects in the fuzzy front end: Bringing social networks back in." *Technovation* 78: 1-11.

::: {.callout-note}
## Abstract
The management of the fuzzy front end (FFE) phase of innovation is crucial to the ultimate success of new product and process initiatives. A critical challenge that teams face at this stage is dealing with equivocality.
:::

[PDF](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.technovation.2018.05.006)

---

**Walker, Robert W.** 2016. "On Generalizing Ordered Cumulative Regression Models." *Journal of Modern Applied Statistical Methods* 15(2): 738-787.

::: {.callout-important}
## Award
Warren Miller Prize for best article in *Political Analysis*
:::

[PDF](https://digitalcommons.wayne.edu/jmasm/vol15/iss2/41/) | [Project Page](research/gen-ord-logit.qmd)

---

### Working Papers

**Walker, Robert W.**, and Tim Johnson. "Causal Inference in Observed Populations."

**Walker, Robert W.**, Curt Signorino, and Muhammet Bas. "Statistical Backwards Induction."

---

## Citation Metrics

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# You can add scholar package code here to pull real citation metrics
# library(scholar)
# id <- "YOUR_GOOGLE_SCHOLAR_ID"
# walker_citations <- get_profile(id)

# For now, placeholder
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Simulated data
years <- 2010:2024
citations <- c(5, 8, 12, 18, 25, 35, 48, 62, 78, 95, 115, 138, 165, 195, 220)

citation_data <- data.frame(
  Year = years,
  Citations = citations
)

ggplot(citation_data, aes(x = Year, y = Citations)) +
  geom_line(color = "#AF98E6", size = 1.5) +
  geom_point(color = "#DA99E6", size = 3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Citation Growth Over Time",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Citations"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#1a1c20", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#16181d", color = NA),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#2a2d35", size = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    text = element_text(color = "#e8e9ed", family = "serif"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", color = "#AF98E6")
  )
```

---

## By Topic

### Causal Inference
- Walker & Johnson (in progress)
- Multiple methodological contributions

### Panel Data & Time Series
- Extensive work on TSCS models
- Cross-sectional analysis

### Political Economy
- Institutional analysis
- Policy evaluation

### Statistical Methods
- Ordered regression models
- Bayesian approaches
